skill.name: Azure Tables SDK for Python
skill.description: Azure Tables SDK for Python (Storage and Cosmos DB). Use for NoSQL key-value storage, entity CRUD, and batch operations. Provides guidance on authentication, table operations, entity management, queries, and batch transactions.
skill.summary: Azure Tables NoSQL key-value storage SDK documentation for Python developers
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Documentation-only skill containing SKILL.md file with Azure Tables SDK usage examples. Static analysis scanned 0 files (0 lines) and found no security issues. This skill provides reference documentation and code examples only - it has no executable code, scripts, or network capabilities. Pure knowledge-base skill for AI-assisted development.

content.user_title: Build Azure Tables integrations with Python
content.value_statement: Developers need guidance on integrating Azure Tables NoSQL storage in Python applications. This skill provides comprehensive documentation on authentication, entity operations, queries, and batch transactions using the Azure Data Tables SDK.
actual_capabilities: Guide users through Azure Tables SDK installation and authentication setup
Provide code examples for table and entity CRUD operations
Explain query filtering and parameterized queries for security
Document batch transaction operations for bulk entity updates
Show async client usage for high-throughput scenarios
limitations: Does not execute actual Azure operations - provides guidance only
No direct Azure subscription or resource management
Cannot create or manage Azure storage accounts
Skill is read-only documentation - no runtime execution

use_cases.title: Python developer building cloud apps
use_cases.description: A Python developer working on Azure cloud applications needs to add NoSQL data storage. This skill guides them through integrating Azure Tables SDK with proper authentication and best practices.

use_cases.title: Data engineer implementing caching layer
use_cases.description: A data engineer needs a fast key-value store for caching or session data. This skill helps them set up Azure Tables with proper partition key design for optimal query performance.
use_cases.target_user: Data engineers implementing caching and session storage

use_cases.title: DevOps engineer setting up infrastructure
use_cases.description: A DevOps engineer needs documentation to help development teams integrate Azure Tables. This skill provides code examples they can share or use as reference documentation.

prompt_templates.title: Getting started with Azure Tables
prompt_templates.prompt: How do I set up authentication with DefaultAzureCredential for Azure Tables SDK in Python?
prompt_templates.scenario: New user setting up Azure Tables for the first time

prompt_templates.title: Entity operations basics
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to create, read, update, and delete entities in Azure Tables using Python SDK.
prompt_templates.scenario: User learning basic entity CRUD operations

prompt_templates.title: Query with filters
prompt_templates.prompt: How do I query entities with filters in Azure Tables? I need to filter by PartitionKey and also by a property like price.
prompt_templates.scenario: User needing to filter query results efficiently

prompt_templates.title: Batch transactions
prompt_templates.prompt: What is the correct way to perform batch operations in Azure Tables? I need to insert multiple entities in the same partition.

output_examples.input: How do I authenticate to Azure Tables using DefaultAzureCredential?
output: Use DefaultAzureCredential for Azure identity-based authentication:
3. Pass credential to TableServiceClient or TableClient
This supports managed identity, service principal, and local development via Azure CLI

output_examples.input: Create a Python function that queries Azure Table for orders in a specific partition
output: Query entities by PartitionKey using query_entities():
1. Use query_filter='PartitionKey eq @pk' for partition queries
3. This pattern prevents injection and optimizes for partition-scoped queries

best_practices: Always query within a single partition when possible - cross-partition queries are slower and more expensive
Use parameterized queries instead of string concatenation to prevent injection attacks
Design partition keys for your query patterns and to ensure even data distribution
anti_patterns: Do not perform cross-partition queries frequently - this causes high latency and costs
Avoid large entities - Azure Tables has a 1MB limit per entity
Do not use RowKey values that are too long - keep them under 1KB for optimal performance

faq.question: What is the difference between TableServiceClient and TableClient?
faq.answer: TableServiceClient manages tables - create, delete, list tables. TableClient works with entities within a specific table - CRUD operations and queries.

faq.question: How do I handle entity keys in Azure Tables?
faq.answer: Every entity requires PartitionKey and RowKey. Together they form a unique identifier. PartitionKey groups related entities for efficient queries.

faq.question: Can I use Azure Tables with Cosmos DB?
faq.answer: Yes, Azure Tables SDK works with both Azure Storage Tables and Cosmos DB Table API. Use the appropriate endpoint URL for each service.

faq.question: How do batch operations work in Azure Tables?
faq.answer: Batch operations use submit_transaction() with a list of operations. All entities must have the same PartitionKey. Maximum 100 operations per batch.

faq.question: What data types are supported in Azure Tables?
faq.answer: Azure Tables support: str, int, float, bool, datetime, bytes, and UUID. Complex types must be serialized to strings.

faq.question: How do I secure my Azure Tables queries?
faq.answer: Use parameterized queries with the parameters argument instead of string formatting. This prevents OData injection attacks.
